title: Cat's Eyes Shaking Back and Forth: Understanding Nystagmus
meta_desc: If your cat's eyes are shaking, they may have nystagmus. Learn why this is a high-priority emergency and how a video can help your veterinarian.
content_html:
TL;DR: Nystagmus is involuntary eye movement indicating an underlying neurological or vestibular issue. Seek immediate veterinary care if your cat's eyes are shaking, especially if they are also stumbling, tilting their head, or vomiting.

What is nystagmus and why are my cat's eyes shaking?

When a cat's eyes shake back and forth, up and down, or in a circular motion, it is a medical condition known as nystagmus. This involuntary eye movement occurs when the brain or the inner ear (the vestibular system) is unable to properly track or stabilize the gaze. While it can be distressing to witness, it is a symptom of an underlying issue rather than a disease itself.

Is my cat having a medical emergency if their eyes are moving involuntarily?

  • The urgency level for nystagmus is High, as it often indicates serious neurological or vestibular problems such as inner ear infections, feline vestibular disease, inflammatory conditions, or toxicity.
  • While not always immediately life-threatening, your cat should be seen by a veterinarian immediately if the eye shaking is accompanied by a head tilt, stumbling, or vomiting.

How can a video of my cat's eye movements help a veterinarian with triage?

  • Capturing a short video ensures the veterinary team sees the exact movement, which is critical since the shaking may be intermittent.
  • A video allows the vet to analyze the direction and speed of the eye movements to help distinguish between a localized ear problem and a more complex brain-related issue.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

When evaluating nystagmus in cats, the animal should be examined for spontaneous nystagmus with the head held in a normal position and in deviated positions (positional nystagmus). Normal vestibular nystagmus (physiologic nystagmus) is seen as a few beats to the left as the head is turned to the left, and to the right as the head is turned to the right. Unilateral lesions of the vestibulocochlear nerves can produce dysequilibrium with a head tilt toward the side of the lesion, along with spontaneous positional, horizontal, or rotary nystagmus. In pendular nystagmus, there is no fast or slow phase, and the nystagmus arcs are similar to the pendulum movement of a clock. A pendular or jerk nystagmus may be seen when the globes are voluntarily moved.

Chapter: Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1216)

faqs.question: Can nystagmus happen suddenly?
faqs.answer: Yes, many cats develop nystagmus very suddenly, often accompanied by a head tilt or difficulty walking.

faqs.question: Is nystagmus painful for my cat?
faqs.answer: The eye movement itself isn't painful, but it causes significant dizziness and nausea, which can be very distressing for your pet.

faqs.question: Can a cat recover from this?
faqs.answer: Recovery depends entirely on the underlying cause. Many vestibular cases improve with supportive care, but prompt diagnosis is essential.
